coredata.dc:description: Bacterial keratitis (BK) is a serious ocular infection and frequently cause irreversible damage to the corneal tissue and even blindness. Current clinical treatments using antibiotics achieve unsatisfactory outcomes due to the lower bioavailability, high risks of drug resistance and limited therapeutic functions. To address these challenges, we proposed a dual-targeted antibacterial and cascaded immunomodulatory therapy strategy, constructing a multifunctional nanoplatform (HPBH@GLA/AMP) using the 3-aminophenylboric acid (NBA) and hyaluronic acid (HA) co-modified hollow polydopamine (HPDA) to serve as a functional nanocarrier (HPBH) for loading antimicrobial peptide (AMP) and glycyrrhizic acid (GLA). The HPBH@GLA/AMP presented excellent targeting and bactericidal performance to Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), coupled with its capacity to alleviate the bacteria-triggered excessive immune response via an ingenious cascaded immunoregulatory process. Notably, the study of underlying anti-inflammatory mechanism revealed that the functional nanocarrier of HPDA could effectively scavenge the reactive oxygen species (ROS) for controlling early inflammatory responses, while inhibit the expression of late mediator high mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) chemokine through released GLA. Specially, the duel-targeted characteristic enabled this nanoplatform to recognize both the bacteria and inflammatory cells precisely and aggregate the location where bacteria and/or inflammatory cells exist persistently, effectively increased the therapeutic concentration of drugs. Featuring the antibacterial synergistic yet sequential regulation for the inflammatory response, the HPBH@GLA/AMP achieved satisfactory therapeutic effects in a BK mouse model in vivo. This study provides a bright prospect for clinical treatment of refractory bacterial keratitis (BK) and even extended to other immunotherapy in infectious disease.

(a) A schematic diagram of dual targeting effects of HPBH@GLA/AMP for bacteria and inflammatory cells. TEM images of P. aeruginosa after the treatment of HPDA (b) and HPBH@GLA/AMP (c) respectively. The representative CLSM images of LPS-stimulated HCECs co-incubated with HPDA (d) and HPBH@GLA/AMP (e). The representative CLSM images of LPS-stimulated HCECs co-incubated with HPDA@Rhodamine B (f) and HPBH@Rhodamine B (g) respectively, and further stained with DAPI (blue).

(a) Representative agar plate photographs for P. aeruginosa after co-incubating with different samples. Histogram of the relative bacteria survival rate (%) corresponding to samples with different concentration of 0.5 mg/mL (b), 1 mg/mL (c), 2 mg/mL (d) and 5 mg/mL (e) (ns, p > 0.05; *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001). (f) CLSM images of P. aeruginosa after treatment with samples with 0.5 mg/mL (scale bar: 20 μm).

(a) Representative CLSM images of HCECs cells co-incubated with different nanoparticles for 24 h (0.5 mg/mL). (b) Cell viability of HCECs cells with different treatments by CCK-8 assay. The hemolysis ratio of different treatments (c) and corresponding photographs of human red blood cell suspensions (d). (a) Illustration of experimental procedures to evaluate antioxidation in the LPS-stimulated HCECs. The corresponding histograms of DPPH free radical scavenging assay of different concentration of HPDA (b) and 0.5 mg/mL of HPDA, HPBH, HPBH@GLA, HPBH@AMP and HPBH@GLA/AM (c) according to the UV–vis absorption spectra. (d) Representative fluorescence images of DCFH-DA in LPS-stimulated HCECs respectively incubated with different concentration of HPDA for 24 h. (e) Representative fluorescence images of DCFH-DA in LPS-stimulated HCECs separately co-cultured with samples (0.5 mg/mL) for 24 h. (ns, p > 0.05; *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001).

(a) Scheme of the experimental procedures to evaluate HMGB1 levels in HCECs. Experiment Ⅰ: HCECs was firstly co-cultured with HPBH@GLA/AMP for 24 h, then added LPS to stimulated with HCECs for 12 h. Experiment ⅠⅠ: The LPS-stimulated HCECs treated with HPBH@GLA/AMP for 24 h. (b) Quantification analysis of HMGB1 in the experiment Ⅰ. (c) Quantification analysis of HMGB1 in experiment ⅠⅠ. Quantitative analysis of mRNA expression levels of HMGB1 (d), TNF-α(e) and IL-6 (f) levels in LPS-stimulated HCECs. (g) Representative image of Flow cytometry analysis of CD80 and F4/80 in RAW264.7 with different nanoparticles treatment and corresponding statistical plot (h). head.ce:title: A multifunctional nanoplatform with dual-targeted antibacterial and cascaded immunomodulatory strategy for the treatment of bacterial keratitis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This multifunctional nanoplatform, featuring the dual recognition for both bacteria and inflammatory cells, could guarantee the local drug concentration furthest. While, equipping with the antibacterial synergistic yet sequential regulation for the inflammatory response, the HPBH@GLA/AMP achieved satisfactory therapeutic effects in a BK mouse model in vivo.

Bacterial keratitis (BK) is a serious ocular infection and frequently cause irreversible damage to the corneal tissue and even blindness. Current clinical treatments using antibiotics achieve unsatisfactory outcomes due to the lower bioavailability, high risks of drug resistance and limited therapeutic functions. To address these challenges, we proposed a dual-targeted antibacterial and cascaded immunomodulatory therapy strategy, constructing a multifunctional nanoplatform (HPBH@GLA/AMP) using the 3-aminophenylboric acid (NBA) and hyaluronic acid (HA) co-modified hollow polydopamine (HPDA) to serve as a functional nanocarrier (HPBH) for loading antimicrobial peptide (AMP) and glycyrrhizic acid (GLA). The HPBH@GLA/AMP presented excellent targeting and bactericidal performance to Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), coupled with its capacity to alleviate the bacteria-triggered excessive immune response via an ingenious cascaded immunoregulatory process. Notably, the study of underlying anti-inflammatory mechanism revealed that the functional nanocarrier of HPDA could effectively scavenge the reactive oxygen species (ROS) for controlling early inflammatory responses, while inhibit the expression of late mediator high mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) chemokine through released GLA. Specially, the duel-targeted characteristic enabled this nanoplatform to recognize both the bacteria and inflammatory cells precisely and aggregate the location where bacteria and/or inflammatory cells exist persistently, effectively increased the therapeutic concentration of drugs. Featuring the antibacterial synergistic yet sequential regulation for the inflammatory response, the HPBH@GLA/AMP achieved satisfactory therapeutic effects in a BK mouse model in vivo. This study provides a bright prospect for clinical treatment of refractory bacterial keratitis (BK) and even extended to other immunotherapy in infectious disease.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite multiple host defense mechanisms inherent in the healthy and intact cornea, the incidence of bacterial keratitis (BK) rises rapidly in recent years due to the widely use of extended-wear contact lenses and the increased ophthalmic surgeries [1,2]. Characterized as a rapidly progressive and aggressive corneal disease, BK has become one of the main causes of visual impairment and blindness and affects up to 1.7 million people per year worldwide [3]. The most current clinical treatments involve the topical administration (such as eye drops or ointments) and intracorneal injection of antibiotics [4]. However, the increasing bacterial resistance toward conventional antibiotics has rendered this class of drugs unsatisfactory outcomes [5]. In particular, BK caused by P. aeruginosa is much more refractory because P. aeruginosa bacteria exhibit natural resistance against antibiotics and tendency to form a stable biofilm which is a typical complex of an extracellular matrix providing the embedded bacterial cells with much stronger drug resistance ability [6]. Moreover, the short precorneal residence time of many commercial ophthalmic antibiotics significantly discount their bioavailability (less than 5 % of the drugs could actually reach the focus area [7–9]) and thus high doses or frequent ocular administration is generally required to fortify the therapeutic effect (as documented, the patients may be required to use the eye drops even every 1 h in some cases [10]) which puts patients at high risks for drug resistance and an exaggerated inflammatory responses [11].

Recent studies point out that some advanced antibacterial methods by means of the development of nanotechnology could achieve quick and effective bacteria elimination, whereas, their therapeutic outcome against infectious keratitis is still moderate due to the narrow focus on antibacterial effect [12,13]. Clinical etiology studies reveal that a plethora of pro-inflammatory factors including tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), nuclear factor-κ-gene binding (NF-κB), and reactive oxygen species (ROS) will immediately converge on the corresponding corneal tissue where the infectious bacteria successfully penetrate [14,15]. Without prompt and efficient regulation for the persistence of these inflammatory mediators, severe inflammatory response and excessive ROS production would operate continuously in the intraocular tissue [16,17]. Specifically, high levels of ROS not only increase cellular oxidative stress to directly induce cell death and corneal injury, but also act as an early messenger leading to the chemotaxis of inflammatory cells and production of inflammatory cytokines which in turn exacerbates the inflammatory response and creates a higher level of ROS as well as ultimately causes irreversible retinal damage and even loss of vision [18,19].

In light of key roles of ROS in the BK pathogenesis, several keratitis therapies integrating antioxidant agents with antibacterial materials have recently received intensive attentions and achieved significant advancements [20]. For instance, Lai et al developed a one-step method to synthesize carbonized nanogels (CNGs) from biogenic quercetin (Qu) and lysine (Lys) as a bifunctional agent with antibacterial and antioxidant properties for topical BK therapy [21]. Benefiting from simultaneous remedying of the damage caused by bacterial infection and induced oxidative stress/inflammation of the cornea, which improved the condition of BK and prevented visual loss. Besides, Chen et al. reported a ciprofloxacin hydrochloride (CIP, a broad-spectrum antibiotic) loaded copolymer vesicle that could perform excellent ROS-scavenging capacity and accurate CIP release at the bacterial infection sites [22]. Taking advantages of the combined antioxidant-antibiotic property, this synergistic strategy demonstrated prominent therapeutic efficacy for BK. Liu et al. constructed a multienzyme-like nanozyme composed of graphdiyne nanosheets and manganese oxide which displayed remarkable antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, providing a better therapeutic effect than commercial ophthalmic voriconazole [23]. Whereas, despite superior efficacy has been shown, these synergistic therapy systems may encounter at least some of the following limitations: (i) the antibiotics-depending bactericidal strategy still cannot evade the challenge of antibiotic resistance; (ii) nanomedicine treatments containing metal or metal oxide nanoparticles have been shown to impose adverse effects on mammalian cells; (iii) more importantly, current anti-inflammatory strategies mainly embodied in strengthening the ROS-scavenging capacity using diverse antioxidant agents, while, strategies capable of sequentially regulating the inflammatory cascade reaction for the treatment of BK have been rarely reported.

High mobility group box 1 (HMGB1), a ubiquitous nuclear protein expressed in virtually all types of cells, plays central role in the pathogenesis of many systemic inflammatory diseases of infectious or sterile origin [24]. Originally described as an intracellular chromatin-binding protein, HMGB1 functions as a structural co-factor for regulating gene transcription among several undertakings. When cells die in a nonprogrammed way (such as necrosis and injury) or immune cells surfer from infectious stimulations, HMGB1 would be passively released from injured cells or actively secreted from sensitized immune cells [25,26]. Excessive quantities of HMGB1 in the extracellular milieu acquire new identities to serve as powerful mediators of inflammation through their own or acting in synergy with other proinflammatory molecules (including LPS, IL-1α, IL-1β, and additional factors). Recently, Hazlett group conducted a series of works revealing a strong correlation between HMGB1 and the severity of P. aeruginosa keratitis [27]. Further, it was demonstrated that treatments based on antagonists (including thrombomodulin and glycyrrhizin) specifically targeting extracellular HMGB1 may sequester its adverse in P. aeruginosa keratitis [28]. In this context, we speculated that the combination of ROS-scavenging and HMGB1-reducing strategy to sequentially regulate the inflammatory cascade reaction for the treatment of BK would probably achieve an excellent and superior prognosis.

Herein, we designed a duel-targeted multifunctional nanoplatform which is expected to exhibit excellent antibacterial and immunomodulatory effects with prolonged resident time in the severely infected areas. Besides, the nanoplatform with hollow nanocarrier could increase the drug loading rate for improving ocular drug bioavailability [29–31]. As shown in Scheme 1 , the 3-aminophenylboric acid (NBA) modified nanoparticle could specifically bond with the lipopolysaccharide (LPS) on the outer membrane of the bacterial cells, and meanwhile highly bactericidal effectiveness could be achieved by releasing the antimicrobial peptide (AMP) in situ. Furthermore, with the co-modification of hyaluronic acid (HA), this nanoparticle could also accurately recognize and bond with the inflammatory cells through the over-expressed CD44 receptor on these cells. Notably, the proposed duel-targeted strategy could effectively improve the accumulation of the nanotherapeutic platform at the inflammatory tissues, and largely reduce the dose frequency. More importantly, by means of the inherent antioxidative capability and the release of glycyrrhizic acid (GLA), this nanoparticle demonstrated sequential regulation for the inflammatory cascade reaction caused by the invading bacteria through scavenging the excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) to alleviate oxidative stress and inhibiting the expression of high mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) chemokine which was proved as a kay late mediator of the inflammatory response in P. aeruginosa related BK. In short, this nanoplatform demonstrates a synergistic therapy combining antibacterial and cascaded immunological properties for preventing the exacerbation of diseases and excessive inflammatory reactions caused by bacterial infections. This strategy is anticipated to provide new insights into the treatment of BK or other related bacterial infections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of HPBH@GLA/AMP NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Hollow polydopamine (HPDA) nanoparticle (NPs), as a functional nanocarrier in this study, was adopted to address the poor solubility of GLA, improve AMP bioavailability as well as perform ROS-scavenging capacity (Fig. 1 a) [32]. HPDA NPs were prepared according to the previous literature [33]. Specially, the preparation of HPDA NPs mainly consisted of two steps: the PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles were obtained by oxidation self-polymerization of dopamine under alkaline condition, then the inner core SiO2 was etched away by hydrofluoric acid (HF) to obtain HPDA NPs. Compared to the smooth surface of SiO2 (Fig. S1), PDA@SiO2 (Fig. 1b) was relatively rough owing to the deposition of PDA and the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) confirmed the hollow structure of the synthesized HPDA (Fig. 1c). Subsequently, HPDA NPs were modified with 3-aminophenylboric acid (NBA) and hyaluronic acid (HA) to obtain HPBA and HPBH NPs respectively via the Schiff base/Michael addition reaction between the amino group and the quinones of HPDA [34,35]. Obviously, this introduction presented any adverse impacts on the morphology and hollow structure of pristine HPDA (Fig. 1d and 1e). Elemental mapping analysis indicated that the B, C, O and N element mainly distributed in the HPBH shell, whereas, the corresponding contents of these elements were sparse in the central area, indicating the hollow structure of HPBH (Fig. 1f). Notably, B was detected in HPBA and HPBH, reflecting NBA was successfully anchored to the HPDA. Further, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the surface chemical composition of nanoparticles. As expected, the wide scan spectrum revealed the presence of B, C, N and O elements (Fig. 1g) and the peak located at 190 eV was observed in HPBH NPs, demonstrating the presence of NBA in HPDA without influence by subsequent HA modifications. Compared to HPDA NPs, XPS analysis exhibited a distinct signal at 285.36 eV in HPBA and HPBH (Fig. 1h), which was assigned to the characteristic peak of −CN– bond [36,37]. Besides, we employed the FTIR to investigate the chemical composition of HPDA, HPBA and HPBH nanoparticles. As was shown in the Fig. S2, the absorption peak at 3300 ∼ 3500 cm−1, attributing to amino groups, became stronger than pristine HPDA, probably due to the surface modification of amino hyaluronic acid (HA) and 3-aminophenylboric acid (NBA) on HPDA nanoparticles [38]. Furthermore, the characteristic peak at 1324 cm−1 in HPBA was ascribed to the B–O group of stepwise NBA conjugation with HPDA [39]. In addition, such characteristic peak could be observed in the HPBH nanoparticles, demonstrating the assembly of NBA on HPDA had no influence on subsequent HA modification to obtain the HPBH nanoparticles. Remarkably, the as-prepared HPBH NPs presented a relatively uniform size of 110 ± 1.6 nm (Fig. 1i) and the zeta-potential was measured to be about −2.7 mV after stepwise NBA and HA conjugation (Fig. 1j) [40,41].

To prevent the visual loss, clinical therapy of severe BK demands simultaneous remedying of the damage caused by the bacterial infection and its induced oxidative stress/inflammation of the cornea. Thus, the GLA and AMP were encapsulated within HPBH NPs and a dual drug delivery system (HPBH@GLA/AMP) were consequentially developed to achieve antibacterial and antioxidant effects separately (Fig. 1a). The drug release profiles of HPBH@GLA/AMP were explored to evaluate the potential for the codelivery of GLA and AMP. Firstly, the released solution of HPBH@GLA/AMP was collected and measured with UV–vis spectra. The characteristic absorption peaks at 258 and 280 nm were discovered, which consisted with pure GLA and AMP absorption peaks. These results proved that the GLA and AMP were loaded into HPBH carrier. Meanwhile, the drug loading capacity of GLA and AMP in HPBH@GLA/AMP were calculated as high as 23.9 ± 3.1 % and 13.9 ± 1.2 % (1 mg/mL), respectively. When the concentration increased to 2 mg/mL, there was no doubt that the release of both drugs was significantly increased. Moreover, the releasing behavior of the GLA and AMP from HPBH@GLA/AMP underwent two different phases both in two concentrations in the Fig. 1k and 1l, which was obtained according to the standard curve (Fig. S3). Taking the 1 mg/mL of HPBH@GLA/AMP as example, an initial burst of GLA and AMP release were observed at the first phase, accounting for the most of total release of GLA and AMP (<10 h). Given that the liquid in eye drops is limited and tend to quickly remove, the premier burst release of the GLA and AMP would be beneficial for combating the bacteria. Of note, the sustained-release process of GLA and AMP was presented at the second phase (10 ∼ 48 h), which can promote the retention of ocular drugs in a lesion to a great extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual targeting effects of HPBH@GLA/AMP for bacteria and inflammatory cells
ce:section.ce:para: Considering eye drops are substantially and rapidly cleared by tears within 30 s of administration, which greatly reduces the therapeutic efficacy of effective constituent [42]. Therefore, dual targeting nanoparticles, HPBH@GLA/AMP were designed and supposed to target bacteria to enhance the antibacterial activity, meanwhile, anchor the inflammatory human corneal epithelial cells (HCECs) for prolonging the retention time of nanoparticles, respectively (Fig. 2 a). As shown in Fig. 2b, only very few HPDA particles (free of any recognized molecules) appeared around the bacteria. In contrast, a large number of HPBH@GLA/AMP NPs were intuitively discovered on the outer membrane of bacteria (Fig. 2c). The cytoderm and cytomembrane of the bacteria can be seen more clearly in the enlarged image, which was presented severe deformation and distinct cytoplasm leakage. While some bacteria maintained their appearance with thick and well-defined cytoderm, internally, the emergence of bubbles indicated irreversible harm. This phenomenon was due to the bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) contains structures of 1,2-diol which are prone to react with borate derivatives of HPBH@GLA/AMP to form borate-diol esters [43]. Furthermore, massive amounts of LPS in Gram-negative bacteria and PGN in Gram-positive bacteria that can activate immune cells to induce excessive inflammation [44,45]. Remarkably, the boron dihydroxyl functional groups of HPBH@GLA/AMP could react with LPS/PGN to inhibit bacterial survival as well as simultaneously neutralize and reduce dead bacteria induced inflammation [46]. Except for the advantage had mentioned, decoration of 3-amino-phenylboronic acid (NBA) on HPBH@GLA/AMP was used to target/anchor bacteria, which can obviously improve the local concentration of drugs in inflammatory tissue and enhance the therapeutic effect of the AMP in HPBH@GLA/AMP NPs. Additionally, the AMP was able to release rapidly from the nanocarrier in the initial stage, accordingly, which was accompanied with a subsequently sustained release, thereby provided a further foundation for the excellent bactericidal effects of HPBH@GLA/AMP.

Severe BK is not only induced by the simple bacterial invading, but the damage caused by oxidative stress/inflammation of the cornea should be addressed. Therefore, the HA of HPBH@GLA/AMP was designed to target the over-expressed CD44 receptor on the inflammatory human cornea and conjunctiva [47,48]. To simulate the HCECs translating into inflammatory state, LPS as a representative inflammatory factor was used to stimulate HCECs, and the obtained inflammatory HCECs were subsequently co-incubated with HPDA or HPBH@GLA/AMP separately. The two-color fluorescence and CLSM were firstly used to evaluate the anchoring ability of nanoparticles on cells. As showed in Fig. 2d and 2e, after co-incubating with the HCECs, there were little HPDA nanoparticles assembling around cells surface than HPBH@GLA/AMP, demonstrating significant targeting and long-standing ability of the composite nanoparticle, which might attribute to the combination of HA with CD44 receptor [49]. To further confirm the anchoring effect on inflammatory tissue, Rhodamine B was loaded into the nanocarrier and obtained particles were treated with the LPS-stimulated HCECs. As shown in Fig. 2f and g, The HPBH@GLA/AMP groups showed more obvious red fluorescent at the HCECs, also pointing HPBH@GLA/AMP composite nanoparticle a better targeting function for inflammation tissue. Therefore, therapeutic strategies leveraged interaction between nanoparticles and cell surface receptors, relying on the recognition of HA with CD44, could achieve better remaining of effective constituents within the anterior corneal tissue than traditional eye drops.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial property of the HPBH@GLA/AMP
ce:section.ce:para: The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) determination was firstly carried out against P. aeruginosa according to a reported method by monitoring the turbidity variation of the bacterial solution via absorbance readings at 600 nm, and the MIC was defined as the lowest nanoparticles concentration that inhibited over 90 % of bacterial growth [50,51]. Briefly, a twofold dilution series of 100 µL nanoparticle solution in LB medium was made on a 96-well microplate, followed by the addition of 100 µL bacterial suspensions (107–108 CFU mL−1). After incubating overnight at 37 °C, the kinetic growth inhibition was determined by optical density (OD) measurements using a microplate reader. As shown in Fig. S4, compared the control group without any nanoparticles, the HPBH@GLA/AMP group exhibited obvious antibacterial activity toward P. aeruginosa after an incubation time for only four hours at 400 µg/mL. While, complete inhibition of bacterial growth could be observed after increasing the HPBH@GLA/AMP concentration for 500 µg/mL. Therefore, the concentration of 500 µg/mL was selected as MIC value for subsequent experiments. To further quantificationally evaluate the in vitro antibacterial performance and distinguish the bactericidal components of the nanoparticles, the plate counting method was performed. As shown in Fig. 3 a, both HPBH@AMP and HPBH@GLA/AMP exhibited excellent inactivation at different concentrations, whereas the bacteria stayed alive in other samples which have no AMP loading regardless of nanoparticle concentrations, indicating the remarkable bactericidal performance of the composite nanoparticles containing AMP. Graphically, the number of survival bacteria of different samples at various concentrations could be calculated based on the total number of bacteria and corresponding percentage of live bacteria obtained from the colony counting method (Fig. 3b–e). It was worth noting that when the concentration of nanoparticles gradually reduced to 0.5 mg/mL, more than 90 % of P. aeruginosa was still eradicated by the HPBH@GLA/AMP. The HPBH@GLA/AMP demonstrated the prominent antibacterial performance, exhibiting a tremendous potential as a highly efficient platform for bactericidal applications. Furthermore, a two-color fluorescence assay by CLSM images of live/dead staining was conducted to test the antibacterial activity of HPBH@GLA/AMP. As shown in Fig. 3f, the HPBH@AMP and HPBH@GLA/AMP displayed almost equivalent essential antibacterial ability that large numbers of dead bacteria, labeled with red fluorescence, were observed. Almost all pathogenic bacteria were killed after co-incubated with HPBH@AMP or HPBH@GLA/AMP, confirming that the AMP loading accounted for the exhibited antibacterial property. Indeed, AMP with a net positive charge can electrostatically interact with negatively charged cell wall components of P. aeruginosa, such as lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and lipoteichoic acid (LTA) [52,53], leading to the membrane destabilization and permeabilization of bacteria. Eventually, the integrity of the bacterial membrane is compromised due to the high osmotic pressure of the cytoplasm, resulting in the cell lysis [54].

Besides, S. aureus is also a representative organism that can cause bacterial keratitis [55–57]. To further evaluate the bactericidal property of this nanoplatform for S. aureus, the MIC value and plate counting methods toward S. aureus were implemented accordingly [58,59]. As displayed in Fig. S5, the MIC of HPBH@GLA/AMP was found to be 400 µg/mL for S. aureus as demonstrated by the fact that there was no obvious variation of the OD values after co-culture for ten hours. In contrast, after increasing the nanoparticle concentration to 500 µg/mL, complete bacterial inhibition could be achieved within only two hours co-culture, signifying that this nanoplatform may be more effective for combating against S. aureus related infections. Similarly, to further confirm this result and figure out the effective constituents of the HPBH@GLA/AMP for killing the S. aureus, the plate counting method was performed. As shown in the Fig. S6, the bacterial inhibiting property displayed by samples without loading of AMP (HPDA, HPBH, HPBH@GLA) at all of tested concentrations was fairly limited in compared with the control group. Notably, after the introduction of AMP, both of HPBH@AMP and HPBH@GLA/AMP at the concentration of 400 µg/mL presented remarkable bactericidal performances that more than 90 % bacteria killing rates were achieved. These phenomena validated that AMP was the essential constitute of the constructed nanoplatform for antibacterial property and 400 µg/mL was the MIC value of HPBH@GLA/AMP for S. aureus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo biocompatibility assay
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility of nanoparticles is a prerequisite since it is directly related to the application and clinical transformation. Therefore, prior to applying as-prepared nanoparticles in the BK in vivo, the cytocompatibility and hemocompatibility of samples were tested. A live/dead staining assay against human corneal epithelial cells (HCECs) was initially conducted to visualize the vitality of cells after the co-culture with nanoparticles. Calcein-AM can be hydrolyzed by endogenous esterase in living cells to produce Calcein, which is a polar molecule with strong negative charge without penetrating the cell membrane, thereby retains in the cell and emits strong green fluorescence. The nucleic acid red fluorescent dye propidium iodide (PI) cannot penetrate the cell membrane of living cells but only stain dead cells whose cell membrane integrity has been destroyed [60]. As shown in Fig. 4 a, there was strong green fluorescence for all samples and no significant evidence of dead cells was observed, indicating negligible toxicity of as-prepared nanoparticles. To further evaluate the potential cytotoxicity of nanoparticles for HCECs quantitatively, the cell counting kit (CCK-8) assay was performed. As shown in Fig. 4b, more than 95 % of HCECs survived after 24 h of incubation even the sample concentration was up to 0.5 mg/mL. Additionally, the in vitro hemolysis assay was employed to verify the hemocompatibility of nanoparticles. As shown in the Fig. 4c and d, the hemolysis ratio of as-prepared nanoparticles was less than 3 %, indicating an excellent blood compatibility. Combining the low cytotoxicity and favorable cell compatibility.

Due to the delicate nature of the eye, the in vivo biocompatibility assay was also conducted to evaluate the ocular irritation caused by nanoparticles. As shown in the Fig. 4e, the corneal epithelium remained intact and there was no conjunctival congestion, increased secretion, or irritated blinking within 24 h of HPBH or HPBH@GLA/AMP treatment. The hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining revealed a clear hierarchical structure of the cornea with no infiltrated inflammatory cells or corneal epithelial defects (Fig. 4f), indicating the excellent safety of the as-prepared HPBH@GLA/AMP nanoparticles. Base on the in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility, the HPBH@GLA/AMP is promising to serve as the potential and safe nanoplatform for in vivo treatment of BK.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immunological cascade regulation of the HPBH@GLA/AMP in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth noting that the pathogenic bacteria invade corneal tissues can attract severe corneal inflammatory responses, such as increasing concentration of ROS [61]. The surge in ROS levels not only triggers lipid peroxidation in iris epithelial cell membranes and photoreceptor mitochondria, which can lead to the chemotaxis of inflammatory cells and irreversible retinal damage [62], but also inactivate anti-apoptotic proteins (e.g. NF-κB) and upregulates endogenous receptor-ligand systems, which in turn, inducing the cell death and inhibiting the normal self-healing function of corneal tissues [63,64]. Thus, in addition to eradicating pathogenic bacteria, protecting corneal tissue from oxidative damage and relieving inflammation are equally critical for the efficient treatment of BK. In this context, current anti-inflammatory strategies mainly embodied in strengthening the ROS-scavenging capacity using diverse antioxidant agents, while, strategies able to combine the ROS-scavenging with the regulation of the inflammatory reaction for the treatment of BK have been rarely reported. In this study, the constructed hollow composite nanoparticles were not only designed to serve as a nanocarrier for loading drugs, but also expect to be capable of efficient ROS-scavenging ability and immunological regulation. Therefore, the extracellular and intracellular ROS scavenging experiment were firstly conducted to verify the ROS-scavenging capability of HPDA (Fig. 5 a). As shown in Fig. 5b, the 2,2-Di (4-tert-octylphenyl)-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) scavenging activity of HPDA increased significantly with the concentration of nanoparticles, demonstrating the sufficient capacity of 96.7 % at a relatively lower concentration (200 μg/mL), originating from the abundant catechol and imine [65,66]. Notably, all samples showed up to 90 % of scavenging rate, also indicating that the subsequent drug loading had no obvious effect on ROS scavenging capacity (Fig. 5c).

Considering subsequent application of the samples in the treatment of BK, the LPS-induced HCECs was established to simulate of the intracellular antioxidant capacity of nanoparticles under inflammatory conditions. The water soluble probe DCFH-DA, which could be oxidized by ROS to produce bright fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCF), was employed to assess the level of ROS [67]. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, the ROS eliminating efficiency followed a concentration-dependent manner of HPDA, and ROS could be efficiently eliminated in the presence of HPDA with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, which was closely related to the antioxidant mechanism of rich quinone structure in the PDA [68]. To further explore the antioxidant ability of HPBH@GLA/AMP, the intrinsic ROS-scavenging ability of different synthesized stages of samples at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL were probed. Different from the control group, all the other groups showed lower green fluorescence of ROS. Meanwhile, comparing with the HPDA, the relative fluorescence intensity of HPBH showed no significant change, which indicated that the surface modification had no effect on the antioxidant capacity (Fig. 5e). Remarkably, HPBH@GLA and HPBH@GLA/AMP groups presented negligible green fluorescence, demonstrating an evident ROS scavenging capacity even at 0.5 mg/mL.

As mentioned above, in addition to focusing on cellular ROS levels, it is worth to underline the strong correlation between proinflammatory factors and the severity of P. aeruginosa keratitis. High mobility group box 1 (HMGB1), belonging to the family of danger-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) or alarmins, can serve as a delayed mediator in inflammatory conditions [69,70]. To be specific, as a latter inflammatory mediator, the extracellular release of HMGB1 will induce inflammatory cascade, aggravate oxidative damage and inhibit tissue angiogenesis in the later stages of BK, rather than intracellular retention in early stage. Therefore, two experiments were designed to simulate the progression of the BK to explore the treatment efficacy of as-prepared nanoparticles (Fig. 6 a). Specifically, on the one hand, HCECs were firstly pretreated with PBS, HPBH, HPBH@GLA and HPBH@GLA/AMP respectively, followed with the LPS stimulation to imitate the early stage of BK that the HMGB1 still stayed inside the cells. As a contrast, another experiment was that LPS was stimulated HCECs firstly to simulate the later inflammatory feature of BK and the resulted HCECs was co-incubated with the above samples (PBS, HPBH, HPBH@GLA and HPBH@GLA/AMP). In this case, the HMGB1 tends to be released outside the cells, thereby abundant extracellular HMGB1 was able to trigger the inflammatory cascade. As shown in Fig. 6b and c, the expression level of HMGB1 in group treated with HPBH (free of loadings) presented significant difference between the two different experiments that compared with the control more than 42.61 % reduction of the concentration could be achieved in the pretreated experiment, while only slight decrease (24.26 %) in the post-dosing experiment. This phenomenon indicated that the HPBH could only inhibit the release of intracellular HMGB1 rather than scavenging the released extracellular HMGB1. In contrast, the HMGB1 in the HPBH@GLA group exhibited the lowest expression than that in other three groups both in two experiments, indicating the superior inhibitory effect of GLA for the release or activation of HMGB1. Notably, the expression levels of HMGB1 in the HPBH@GLA group presented similar tendency that obvious lower concentration was observed in the pretreated group compared with post-dosing group, proofing that the nanoplatform containing the drug of GLA could perform a prophylactic effect on the release of HMGB1. Thus, it is such prevention of extracellular release of HMGB1 that can restrain the mediator of inflammation and avoid the subsequent inflammatory cascade.

To further clarify the cascaded immunoregulatory signaling pathway involvement of the composite HPBH@GLA/AMP nanoparticle, quantitative real-time RT-PCR analysis was conducted to test the mRNA expressions of HMGB1 and the related pro-inflammatory cytokines including tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α) and interleukin-6 (IL-6) in LPS-stimulated HCECs. As shown in the Fig. 6d, compared with the control, HPBH, HPBH@GLA, and HPBH@GLA/AMP achieved effective suppression of HMGB1 that was consistent with the ELISA results. In contrast to HMGB1 as a late mediator of the inflammatory response, TNF-α was released by activated immune cells early in the disease response, playing a vital role in the formation of chronic BK through the induction of ocular inflammation to disrupt the barrier function of corneal epithelial cells [71,72]. Thus, the estimation of TNF-α related mRNA expression might be an important indicator for evaluating the progress of BK. As displayed in the Fig. 6e, without the loading of GLA, the nanocarrier HPBH alone treated group could achieve inhibition for the expression of TNF-α, which might be attributed to the performance for eliminating the early massive ROS that was related to the secretion of TNF-α. Obviously, after the loading of GLA, the TNF-α expressions in the groups of HPBH@GLA, HPBH@GLA/AMP were slightly higher than that of the HPBH group, confirming the effectiveness of both the ROS scavenging and inhibiting the expression of late mediator HMGB1 could obviously inhibit TNF-α expression. Notably, the HPBH and HPBH@GLA/AMP possessed prominent inhibition of IL-6 mRNA transcription (Fig. 6f). As an important member of the cytokine network, IL-6 plays a central role in acute inflammatory responses [73,74], thus such superior inhibition property of inflammatory biomarkers might originated from both the ROS scavenging and HMGB1 reduction, following by blocking the NF-κB pathway [75,76]. Therefore, it is essential to coordinate ROS and HMGB1, which was served as early messenger and late inflammatory response mediator in BK respectively, to realize efficient immunological cascade therapy.

Moreover, the invading of bacteria is not only marked by the inflammatory epithelial edema arised from the inflammatory response of corneal epithelial cells but also induces severe infiltration of the stroma by the recruitment of polymorphonuclear cells (PMNs) and mononuclear cells. Wherein, macrophages play an important part in the refractory keratitis by maintaining the M1 phenotype to inhibit inflammation resolution [77,78]. Therefore, the RAW264.7 cells selected as the representative model of immune cell were stimulated by the LPS for 24 h to initiate the immune response. After co-culture with samples the transformation of their phenotype was monitored through the flow cytometry. As displayed in Fig. 6g and h, evident reduction for the proportion of M1 macrophage was observed after the treatment of HPBH (without any contents inside), suggesting that the nanocarrier alone could inhibit the macrophage transform to the proinflammatory M1 phenotype to a certain degree taking advantages of its inherent ROS-scavenging ability. After the introduction of GLA, the HPBH@GLA (without the loading of AMP) treated group exhibited the lowest proportion of M1 macrophage, indicating that the GLA related HMGB1 downregulating could further suppress the primary macrophages to M1 state. The slight difference between HPBH@GLA and HPBH@GLA/AMP for RAW264.7 cells regulation was ascribed to introduction of AMP, which might influence the effect of single GLA components. Thus, it was suspected that the ROS scavenging and simultaneously counteraction for HMGB1 in HPBH@GLA/AMP NPs was also important for modulating macrophage polarization, possessing potentially inhibition of the activation of immune cells caused by bacterial infection in BK. In conclusion, the underlying mechanisms for bacteria-induced BK treatment with HPBH@GLA/AMP NPs through ROS scavenging and HMGB1 reduction might be explained as follows (Fig. 6i). Under the bacterial-induced infectious microenvironment, the functional nanocarrier HPBH of HPBH@GLA/AMP NPs displayed strong antioxidation effects through excellent ROS-scavenging ability, which resulted in the suppression of the early inflammatory factor of TNF-α. In terms of the late mediator of the inflammatory response, the released GLA could greatly inhibit the HMGB1 chemo-attractant and mitogenic activities to quit excessively extracellular HMGB1 to bond with RAGE for counteracting the chemokine and cytokine mediated inflammatory cascade. Afterwords, HPBH@GLA/AMP could restrain the inflammatory cytokines NF-κB to further downregulate the IL-6, which relayed on the synergy of low expression of HMGB1 and TNF-α. As a results, the multifunctional nanoplatform (HPBH@GLA/AMP) can significantly alleviate BK through inhibit the occurrence of inflammatory storms by immunological cascade therapy. These findings demonstrated potential value for the advancement of ophthalmic medications and the treatment of ocular inflammatory diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo synergistic efficacy of HPBH@GLA/AMP for refractory BK
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the efficacy of in vitro antibacterial activity and immunological cascade regulation, the in vivo synergistic efficacy of HPBH@GLA/AMP in treating BK was verified through a mouse model, which was established by injecting P. aeruginosa suspension (108 CFU/mL, 10 μL) to infect the wound on the mice cornea according to an international general modeling method [79]. After 24 h of inoculation, the experimental BK model was grouped into five groups, which topically received 6 μL of PBS (as the control), HPBH, HPBH@GLA and HPBH@GLA/AMP three times a day, respectively, followed by slit lamp observation, bacterial plate counting assay, hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining of corneal sections and immunohistochemistry to assess the therapeutic effect of different treatments (Fig. 7 a). In the slit lamp images, left images displayed the extent and area of corneal opacity and surface regularity, whereas sodium fluorescein staining images were presented in the right row to visualize the degree of corneal defects (Fig. 7b). Thus, the severity of BK can be graded base above mentioned images (Fig. 7c). In the control group, the infection was extremely severe (clinical grading scale: 10.66 ± 0.57) that muddiness and purulence were observed at the central cornea after 3 days of PBS treatment. Moreover, the slit-lamp examination scores and corneal thickness values rapidly increased. In the fluorescent images, clinical manifestations of infections such as significant clouding and stromal edema were visible within the cornea and the corneal defect. The corneal stromal infiltration and haze in the HPBH@GLA group (clinical grading scale: 7.33 ± 1.15) can be observed but less than HPBH groups (clinical grading scale: 9.66 ± 0.57), suggesting that the single antioxidation of HPBH by scavenging ROS was not as effective as HPBH@GLA, which possessed additional HMGB1-reduction property to sequentially regulate the inflammatory cascade. In sharp contrast, the clinical grading scale was 4.33 ± 0.57 in the HPBH@GLA/AMP group, revealing that the corneal infection was almost recovered with no chemosis stromal infiltrate relying on reconciliation of excellent bactericidal effect of AMP based on immunological cascade therapy of HPBH@GLA. Besides, the corneal tissues from each group after three days of treatment were surgically excised and the bacterial number was determined via an agar plate counting assay to quantitatively evaluate the antibacterial activity of HPBH@GLA/AMP against P. aeruginosa infections (Fig. 7d). Compared with the control group, the HPBH and HPBH@GLA groups exhibited moderate antimicrobial activities. In comparison, the HPBH@GLA/AMP showed a distinct bactericidal performance due to the introduction of AMP (Fig. 7e). In a word, these results suggested that the HPBH@GLA/AMP could control infection to relief the symptoms, realizing remarkable therapeutic efficacy in BK.

Subsequently, the hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining and immunohistochemical staining were performed to localize the expression of related inflammatory cytokines and detailly analyze the therapeutic effect of samples. As shown in Fig. 8 a, severe infiltration of inflammatory cells and a significant thickening of the corneal structure as well as distinct corneal edema with an irregular stromal stroma were observed in both control and HPBH alone groups, indicating that only single antioxidation played negligible therapeutic effect on the refractory BK. After the introduction of GLA that has been proved to inhibit the expression of HMGB1, the situation of the cornea was obviously improved as demonstrated by scattered inflammatory cell distribution and significant decrease in the cornea edema, suggesting that the cascade immunoregulatory effects of HPBH@GLA nanoparticles could still be well achieved in vivo and provided better anti-inflammatory outcomes than HPBH treat alone. Notably, almost no infiltration of inflammatory cells and a neat/preserved corneal epithelium layer were observed in the group treated with HPBH@GLA/AMP, demonstrating that the constructed composite nanoplatform combining the bactericidal property with cascade immunoregulatory effects was an effective strategy against the refractory BK. Subsequently, the typical BK related proinflammatory cytokines including HMGB1, COX-2, TNF-α and IL-6 were detected by immunohistochemical staining (IHC staining) to reconfirm anti-inflammatory properties of the composite nanoplatform. Therein, TNF-α is an essential characteristic indicator for the host inflammatory response, and IL-6 is a pleiotropic cytokine that plays a significant role in the regulation of various host immune responses. As shown in the Fig. 8b and c, proinflammatory cytokines including HMGB1 and COX-2 were both highly expressed in the control and HPBH alone treated groups, indicating that the antioxidant property alone was incapable to control the progress of BK. Further, although reduced expressions of all proinflammatory cytokines were verified in the HPBH@GLA treated group, the expressions of these proinflammatory cytokines were still at higher levels compared with the HPBH@GLA/AMP treated group where the expression of proinflammatory cytokines was almost negligible (Fig. 8d and e), confirming that it was essential to combine the anti-inflammatory property with antibacterial performance for dealing with BK. Taken together, these results demonstrated the effective therapeutic effects of HPBH@GLA/AMP on BK in vivo, and the strategy of chemo-antibacterial/immunological cascade therapy might be a prospective therapeutic strategy for the management of BK.

In this work, we successfully constructed a multifunctional nanoplatform combining dual-targeted antibacterial therapy and cascaded immunomodulatory, providing an innovative and promising approach for combating refractory bacterial keratitis. While traditional methods often focused on periodical antibiotic administration to process the bacterial infection, this work provided a holistic therapeutic strategy which dealt with the bacterial invasion and sequentially regulated early/lately inflammatory messenger, realizing satisfactory therapeutic effects ultimately. This multifunctional nanoplatform leveraged the inherent antibacterial property of AMP; while, scavenging the ROS for controlling early inflammatory responses, followed by inhibiting the expression of late mediator HMGB1 chemokine through released GLA. Furthermore, featuring the recognition for both bacteria and inflammatory cells, the HPBH@GLA/AMP NPs could guarantee the local drug concentration furthest, thereby achieving competent drugs delivery effect. Overall, this study provided valuable insights for clinical treatment of refractory bacterial keratitis and opened the door for cascaded immunotherapy in numerous infectious diseases.

Dopamine (DA), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS > 99 %), ammonia solution (NH3·H2O), hydrogen fluoride (HF), 3-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride (NH2-NBA), hyaluronic acid (HA), glycyrrhizic acid (GLA), antimicrobial peptide (AMP, HHC36: KRWWKWWRR) were purchased from Dangang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). Human corneal epithelial cell line (HCECs) were obtained from the cell bank of the Chinese academy of science (Shanghai, China). Mouse macrophage cells (RAW 264.7) were purchased from Zhong Qiao Xin Zhou Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) (ATCC 27853) were purchased from Qingdao Green Valley Biotechnological Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). A live/dead baclight bacterial viability kit (L-7012) was obtained from molecular probe Inc (USA). The cell live/dead staining kit was purchased form beyotime biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was provided from yeasen biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2,2-Di(4-tert-octylphenyl)-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) was purchased form Aladdin Holdings Group Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI) were purchased from Elabscience Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Human High mobility group box 1 (HMGB-1) ELISA Kit was obtained from Jianglai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anti-GAPDH antibody, anti-PGHS-2 antibody and anti-HMGB1 antibody were purchased form Proteintech Group Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). ChamQ Universal SYBR qPCR Master Mix, HiScript III All-in-one RT SuperMix perfect for qPCR and TransZol Up Plus RNA Kit were obtained from Vazyme Biotech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). H-TNF-α-R, H-TNF-α-F, H-IL-6-R, H-IL-6-F, H-HMGB1-R, H-HMGB1-F, H-GADPH-R and H-GADPH were obtained from atantares Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Firstly, silica (SiO2) nanoparticles were synthesized through the hydrolysis of Tetraethyl tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS). Briefly, anhydrous ethanol (350 mL), deionized water (10 mL) and NH3·H2O (7.5 mL) were added together and stirred uniformly for 5 min. Then, 15 mL of TEOS was added to the solution and stirred for 6 h to obtain SiO2. Subsequently, the mixed solution was washed with methanol to remove excess unreactive substances to acquire SiO2 nanoparticles. Thereafter, the acquired SiO2 was redispersed in Tris buffer (10 mM, pH = 8.5), and DA solution were added stirred for 72 h at room temperature to obtain PDA@SiO2. To acquire the hollow PDA (denoted as HPDA), the PDA@SiO2 was redispersed in Tris buffer solution (3.3 mg/mL) and added 600 μL HF stirred for 1 h at room temperature. After washing with ultrapure water and freeze drying, the HPDA nanoparticles were collected. Subsequently, 18.8 mg HPDA was dissolved in fresh Tris buffer (15 mL, 10 mM, pH = 8.5), followed by adding 15 mL aqueous solution of NH2-NBA and stirred for 24 h to obtain NH2-NBA modified HPDA (denoted as HPBA). Then the acquired HPBA was redispersed in 15 mL of deionized water, and 15 mL of NH2-HA solution (1 mg/mL) was added and stirred at the room temperature for 24 h. The composite nanoparticles decorated with NBA and HA were obtained (denoted as HPBH).

For loading of the GLA and AMP, the HPBH (5 mg/mL) was dispersed in 20 mL PBS solution containing 100 mg GLA followed by continuous stirring for 24 h in vacuum. Then, the supernatant of the mixture was removed by centrifugation to obtain HPBH@GLA. The acquired HPBH@GLA was redispersed in 20 mL mixed water solution containing 100 mg AMP immediately after continuous stirring for 24 h in vacuum, washing with ultrapure water and freeze drying, the HPBH@GLA/AMP nanoparticles were collected.

The microstructure of the as-dissolved materials was characterized using a scanning electron microscope (TESCAN MIRA LMS, Czech Republic) and a transmission electron microscope (HT7800, HITACHI, Japan) and (JEOL JEM-2100Plus). The surface chemical compositions of nanoparticles were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). The surface zeta potentials of the samples were tested using a Solid Surface Zeta Potential Analyzer (Malvern: Nano ZS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro release profiles of GLA and AMP
ce:section.ce:para: The nanoparticles were dispersed in PBS (pH = 7.4) and placed in the shaker to imitate drug release. The corresponding standard curve were established by the UV–vis spectrum test for the GLA and AMP release assay. The HPBH@GLA/AMP nanoparticles were immersed in 2 mL PBS (pH = 7.4) at 37 °C and shaken at 120 rpm. At regular intervals, 2 mL of aliquots were withdrawn from the release media for UV–vis analysis and replaced with an equal volume of fresh buffer solution to maintain a constant volume. The cumulative amount of GLA and AMP released from nanoparticles in each buffer were determined by the corresponding absorbances at 258 and 280 nm, separately. The release content and ratio of each test were calculated to draw the release curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual targeting effects of HPBH@GLA/AMP NPs
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the function of targeting cells, HCECs were cultured at 37 °C under 5 % CO2 with Dulbecco modified Eagle medium (DMEM)-F12 supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum, which were seeded in a six-well cell culture plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells/mL and cultured for 24 h. Then, LPS (8 μg/mL) were added and co-cultured for 12 h to obtain LPS-stimulated HCECs. Subsequently, the LPS-stimulated HCECs was co-incubated with HPDA and HPBH@GLA/AMP NPs (500 μg/mL) for 4 h. Finally, the HCECs were observed by Confocal microscopy (EVOS M7000). To further determined the function of anchoring cells, Rhodamine B was loaded into the nanocarrier of HPBH to enable molecular imaging of the nanoparticles at the cell. To prepare HPBH@Rhodamine B, 0.05 g of Rhodamine B was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and stirred uniformly for 5 min. Then, 0.1 g HPBH were added into Rhodamine B solution system and stirred for 24 h in vacuum to obtain HPBH@ Rhodamine B NPs. Subsequently, the LPS-stimulated HCECs also was co-incubated with HPDA and HPBH@GLA/AMP NPs (500 μg/mL) for 4 h. Finally, the HCECs were observed by Confocal microscopy (EVOS M7000).

Besides, to verify the function of anchoring bacterial, 50 μL of HPDA and HPBH@GLA/AMP (500 μg/mL) were mixed with 150 μL of P. aeruginosa suspensions (107 CFU/mL), respectively. The collected bacteria were fixed with glutaraldehyde for 4 h, then, fixing with osmic acid and glutaraldehyde, dehydrated in ethanol, and embedded in Epon812 resin. Finally, the bacterial sections were observed under a TEM instrument (JEOL JEM-2100Plus).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial property of the HPBH@GLA/AMP
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the antibacterial of samples for P. aeruginosa, 50 μL of HPDA, HPBH, HPBH@GLA, HPBH @AMP and HPBH@GLA /AMP (500 μg/mL) was mixed with 150 μL of P. aeruginosa suspensions (107 CFU/mL), respectively. Afterward, 100 μL of the diluted bacterial solution was uniformly coated on a pre-prepared agar plate, which was incubated in an incubator at 37 °C for 18 h. Finally, the total number of live bacteria was determined by counting the number of colonies. The log reduction in the number of visible bacteria (reduction of log scale) was calculated to evaluate the viability of bacteria. In the bacterial live/dead fluorescent assays, Syto 9 (5 μL) and PI (5 μL) were mixed with the treated bacterial suspension (the treatment method was the same as above), incubated for 15 min, and observed under a fluorescence microscope (Zeiss-Celldiscoverer 7, Germany).

As for the MIC values of HPBH@GLA /AMP toward P. aeruginosa and S. aureus, 100 μL of the bacterial solutions (108 CFU/mL) were added to 100 μL liquid LB medium supplemented with 300, 400, 500, 600 and 700 mg/mL of HPBH@GLA /AMP. After incubating overnight at 37 °C, the kinetic growth inhibition was determined by optical density (OD) measurements using a microplate reader at specific time intervals.

To further test the antibacterial property of samples for S. aureus, 50 μL of HPDA, HPBH, HPBH@GLA, HPBH @AMP and HPBH@GLA /AMP (500 μg/mL) were mixed with 150 μL of S. aureus suspensions (107 CFU/mL), respectively. Afterward, 150 μL of the diluted bacterial solution was uniformly coated on a pre-prepared agar plate, which was incubated in an incubator at 37 °C for 16 h. Finally, the total number of live bacteria was determined by counting the number of colonies. The bacterial reduction in the number of visible bacteriawas calculated to evaluate the viability of bacteria.

To evaluate the Cytotoxicity of nanoparticles, cell viability was measured via a CCK-8 assay. In brief, 1 × 104 cells/well of HCECS were seeded in 96-well microplates and cultured for 24 h. The HCECs were subsequently cultured with samples for 24 h. Then, cells were incubated with the CCK-8 solution for 4 h. The optical density (both the experimental and blank groups) of the incubated cells was determined using a multifunctional microplate reader at 450 nm. In the cell live/dead fluorescent assays, 1 × 104 cells/well of HCECS were seeded in 96-well microplates and cultured with samples for 24 h, which were subsequently incubated with Calcein-AM (5 μM) and propidium iodide (10 μM) for 30 min. After being washed by PBS, macrophages were imaged by fluorescence microscopy.

As for biocompatibility assay, Red blood cells (RBCs) were separated from serum by centrifugation (1000 rpm, 15 min), and then washed three times with normal saline solution and diluted 10 folds to prepare RBCs suspension. Positive control (PC) and negative control (NC) were prepared by ultrapure water and normal saline, respectively. Equal volume of RBCs suspension and different samples were mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h and the blood mixture was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the upper supernatant was transferred into 96-well plate for absorbance measurement at 540 nm by microplate reader. The hemolysis ratio was calculated using the formula below:

To assess the ocular tolerability of the HPBH@GLA /AMP eye drops as previously. In brief, 10 μL of HPBH and HPBH@GLA /AMP were instilled into right eyes three times a day, respectively. The ocular manifestation was evaluated using a slit lamp after being treated for 24 h and epithelial integrity was assessed by fuorescein staining at 24 h postinstillation. Afterwards, the eyeballs were harvested and subjected to histopathological examination using hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining. Meanwhile, healthy mice was designed as control group.

To evaluate the antioxidant activities of samples, the DPPH scavenging activities (%) were designed. Specifically, different concentrations of HPDA (2 mL, 0, 50, 100, 200 μg/mL) with DPPH ethanol solution (1 mL, 1 mM) were incorporated into the water to obtain 4 mL solution system. Then, the obtained mixtures were centrifuged (6000 rpm, 15 min) after the reaction for 30 min (in the darkness). Then, the full wavenumber scanning curves were recorded using UV–vis spectrophotometer. The relative DPPH radical scavenging ratios were calculated by the following formula:

where A0, At were the maximum absorption of the blank (DPPH + ethanol) and the maximum absorption of the hydrogel (DPPH + ethanol + nanoparticles) at different times, respectively. The nanoparticles 0 mg is defined as the control group, and the DPPH scavenging ratio of the control group is 0 %.

To evaluate the ability of intracellular ROS elimination: HCECs were seeded in a six-well cell culture plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells/mL and cultured with samples for 24 h. The cells were then stimulated with LPS (8 μg/mL) for 12 h to simulated inflammatory state, next, treating with samples for 24 h. The cells were then rinsed with PBS three times and stained with DCFH-DA (5 μM) for 15 min at 37 °C. Eventually, the HCECs were observed by Confocal microscopy (EVOS M7000).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Human high mobility group box 1 (HMGB-1) ELISA kit
ce:section.ce:para: HMGB-1ELISA Kit was used to quantitative analysis the efficacy of reducing HMGB1. To be specific, HCECs were seeded in a 6-well cell culture plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells/mL and cultured for 24 h. The cells were stimulated with LPS (8 μg/mL) for 12 h to obtain LPS-stimulated HCECs, then treated with samples (500 μg/mL) for 24 h. ELISA kits were used to quantify HMGB-1 protein levels. Each well was charged with 100 µL of the sample solution, and the absorbance was determined at 450nm using a reference wavelength of 630nm.
RNA extraction, reverse transcription and cDNA PCR amplification were completed by conventional methods [80]. The primer sequences used in the research were as follows:
5′-CATGTTCGTCATGGGTGTGAA-3′ (GADPH forward),
5′-GGCATGGACTGTGGTCATGAG-3′ (GADPH reverse);
5′-CACAAGTCCGGAGAGGAGAC-3′ (IL-6 forward),
5′-CAGAATTGCCATTGCACAAC-3′ (IL-6nreverse);
5′-ACCCTCACACTCAGATCATCTT-3′ (TNF-α forward),
5′-GGTTGTCTTTGAGATCCATGC-3′ (TNF-α reverse);
5′-CTCAGAGAGGTGGAAGACCATGT-3′ (HMGB1 forward),
5′-GGTGCATTGGGATCCTTGAA-3′ (HMGB1 reverse).

RAW264.7 cells were seeded in a 96-well cell culture plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells/mL and cultured for 24 h. The cells were stimulated with LPS (100 ng/mL) for 12 h, then treated with samples (500 μg/mL) for 24 h. Total macrophages were labeled with rabbit F4/80 polyclonal primary antibody (M1 macrophages were labeled with rabbit CD80 polyclonal primary antibody). The samples were subsequently analyzed using flow cytometry (Beckman cytoflex, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo mice subcutaneous infection model
ce:section.ce:para: Female C57BL/6 J mice (6–8 weeks old) were purchased from Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. The study protocols were approved by the Ethics Committee of Eye hospital of Shandong First Medical University (Eye hospital of Shandong First Medical University). The mice were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 0.06 % sodium pentobarbital. Then, 10 µL of P. aeruginosa solution (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was dripped on the corneal. After 48 h of incubation, the established P. aeruginosa keratitis models were randomly divided into five groups, 3 µL samples drops (500 µg/mL) were administered topically three times per day for three consecutive days. Photographs of the mouse eyes at Days 0, 1, 2, 3 were obtained with a slit-lamp microscope. After the mice were humanely euthanized, the eyeballs were removed for histological analysis, immunohistochemical staining detection.

In all experiments, at least 3 different parallel samples were selected. And each assay was carried out for at least 3 times to ensure the dependability of the result. The report data were the mean values of triplicate specimens. Statistical analysis was performed by the analysis of variance (ANOVA) method. In all evaluations, p < 0.05 was considered as statistically significant, and the data were indicated with (*) for p < 0.05, (**) for p < 0.01, and (***) for p < 0.001, respectively.
